claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 17 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask:

Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex actually left school recently, but they've just registered with the careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful information. Can you tell me approximately when Alex left education and registered with the careers service? I need to understand the timeline to determine eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been a few months now since Alex left school and signed up with the careers service. They're still trying to work out their next steps.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to clarify one more important detail:

Can you tell me more specifically how many weeks or months ago Alex left education? Is it more than 20 weeks ago?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, it hasn't been that long - it's been less than 20 weeks. Still within that period where they're getting sorted with the careers service.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: According to Child Benefit rules, when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service, Child Benefit can continue for up to 20 weeks. Since Alex is 17, has left education less than 20 weeks ago, and has registered with the careers service, you remain eligible for Child Benefit during this 20-week period.

**Important to note:** Once the 20-week period ends, your eligibility will depend on whether Alex enters approved education, training, or employment. If Alex starts working 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training, Child Benefit would stop. However, if Alex enters approved education or training, you could continue to receive Child Benefit until they turn 20.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and left education recently but has registered with a government-sponsored careers service. According to the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex left education less than 20 weeks ago and has registered with the careers service, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex during this 20-week period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who lives with them. Alex left education less than 20 weeks ago and has registered with the careers service. Under Child Benefit rules, when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks. Since it has been less than 20 weeks, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since it has been less than 20 weeks, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
